tossups.question: A critic from this country who quipped “Fish are born to fly, and everywhere they swim” is the subject of Aileen Kelly’s The Discovery of Chance. A critic from this country wrote a harsh letter that claims the public is forgiving for a bad book “but never for a pernicious one.” The memoir My Past and Thoughts is by a writer from this country who coined the phrase “Genghis Khan with the telegraph.” A collection of essays on this country’s (*) 19th-century thinkers inspired Tom Stoppard to portray the editor of The Contemporary in The Coast of Utopia. An author from this country failed to express “one big thing” in his theory of history according to Isaiah Berlin. For 10 points, “The Hedgehog and the Fox” comments on a Christian anarchist from what country who wrote Resurrection, The Kreutzer Sonata, and War and Peace?
tossups.question_sanitized: A critic from this country who quipped "Fish are born to fly, and everywhere they swim" is the subject of Aileen Kelly's The Discovery of Chance. A critic from this country wrote a harsh letter that claims the public is forgiving for a bad book "but never for a pernicious one." The memoir My Past and Thoughts is by a writer from this country who coined the phrase "Genghis Khan with the telegraph." A collection of essays on this country's (*) 19th-century thinkers inspired Tom Stoppard to portray the editor of The Contemporary in The Coast of Utopia. An author from this country failed to express "one big thing" in his theory of history according to Isaiah Berlin. For 10 points, "The Hedgehog and the Fox" comments on a Christian anarchist from what country who wrote Resurrection, The Kreutzer Sonata, and War and Peace?
tossups.answer: Russia [or the Russian Empire or Rossiya or Rossiyskaya Imperiya; accept Russian Thinkers] (The unnamed authors are Leo Tolstoy, Alexander Herzen, and Vissarion Belinsky, who are all the subject of long essays in Berlin’s Russian Thinkers. The second sentence refers to Belinsky’s letter to Gogol.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Russia [or the Russian Empire or Rossiya or Rossiyskaya Imperiya; accept Russian Thinkers] (The unnamed authors are Leo Tolstoy, Alexander Herzen, and Vissarion Belinsky, who are all the subject of long essays in Berlin's Russian Thinkers. The second sentence refers to Belinsky's letter to Gogol.)

tossups.question: A play by this author features a deck of cards called “Adelaida Ivanovna” by a character who is tricked by “the Glovs.” A character claims to be the author of The Marriage of Figaro in a play by this author, who also wrote about Podkolyosin jumping out a window to avoid the title event. The declaration, “What are you laughing about? You are laughing at yourselves!” appears in a play by this author that ends with all characters staying (*) frozen for a minute and a half. The squires Bobchinsky and Dobchinsky appear in that play, which sees the protagonist flirt with Anton’s wife and daughter. Osip helps trick the Governor in that play by this author, in which members of a town offer bribes to Khlestakov. For 10 points, name this Russian playwright of The Inspector General.
tossups.question_sanitized: A play by this author features a deck of cards called "Adelaida Ivanovna" by a character who is tricked by "the Glovs." A character claims to be the author of The Marriage of Figaro in a play by this author, who also wrote about Podkolyosin jumping out a window to avoid the title event. The declaration, "What are you laughing about? You are laughing at yourselves!" appears in a play by this author that ends with all characters staying (*) frozen for a minute and a half. The squires Bobchinsky and Dobchinsky appear in that play, which sees the protagonist flirt with Anton's wife and daughter. Osip helps trick the Governor in that play by this author, in which members of a town offer bribes to Khlestakov. For 10 points, name this Russian playwright of The Inspector General.
tossups.answer: Nikolai Gogol (the first line refers to The Gamblers; the unnamed play in the second line is Marriage)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nikolai Gogol (the first line refers to The Gamblers; the unnamed play in the second line is Marriage)

tossups.question: At the end of a story by this author, a bard sings of a knight that cursed the descendants of his evil brother, whose gigantic body writhes under a mountain. In another story by this author, the protagonist recovers from his yearslong amnesia and throws an ax at an old woman, who had earlier convinced him to decapitate a boy to receive a chest of gold buried near a red fern. This author’s story “The Terrible Vengeance” appears in a collection narrated by the (*) beekeeper Rudy Panko. This author’s story “St. John’s Eve” features a witch, as does his later story “Viy” (“vee”). A ghost haunts the Kalinkin bridge and steals pieces of clothing in a story by this author of the collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka. For 10 points, name this Ukrainian author of “The Overcoat.”
tossups.question_sanitized: At the end of a story by this author, a bard sings of a knight that cursed the descendants of his evil brother, whose gigantic body writhes under a mountain. This author's story "The Terrible Vengeance" appears in a collection narrated by the (*) beekeeper Rudy Panko. This author's story "St. John's Eve" features a witch, as does his later story "Viy" ("vee"). A ghost haunts the Kalinkin bridge and steals pieces of clothing in a story by this author of the collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka. For 10 points, name this Ukrainian author of "The Overcoat."

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a man insults his future stepmother by calling her 100 years old, then cuts off her retorts by throwing mud at her. A character created by this author invents a ruse to marry a farmer's daughter involving a cursed jacket that is heralded by the appearance of a pig's head. At the end of a story by this author, a ghost shakes his fist at a timid watchman, having earlier haunted a person of importance who had scolded a clerk out of his office. This author included "The Fair at Sorochyntsi ("sah-rah-CHIN-seh") in the collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka. This author wrote a story in which the copyist Akaky Akakievich obsessively saves money to buy a new garment. For 10 points, name this Russian author of "The Overcoat."
tossups.question_sanitized: In a story by this author, a man insults his future stepmother by calling her 100 years old, then cuts off her retorts by throwing mud at her. A character created by this author invents a ruse to marry a farmer's daughter involving a cursed jacket that is heralded by the appearance of a pig's head. At the end of a story by this author, a ghost shakes his fist at a timid watchman, having earlier haunted a person of importance who had scolded a clerk out of his office. This author included "The Fair at Sorochyntsi ("sah-rah-CHIN-seh") in the collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka. This author wrote a story in which the copyist Akaky Akakievich obsessively saves money to buy a new garment. For 10 points, name this Russian author of "The Overcoat."

tossups.question: A story by this author ends after a hungover man who promised to host a dinner party is found by his angry guests hiding in the title vehicle. In a story by this author, a man becomes a monk to purify his soul after painting a religious scene in which every figure had demonic eyes. In that story by this author, a painter abandons his style and paints for aristocrats after a bag of money is found in the frame of a cursed portrait of a moneylender. Near the end of a story by this author, a cobbler named (*) Hoffmann helps beat up an officer for dancing with the wife of a tinsmith named Schiller. This author of the collection Arabesques wrote a story in which a painter who takes opium to dream of a dark-haired prostitute is contrasted with Lieutenant Pirogov, who followed a blonde woman down the title street. For 10 points, name this author who included the story “Nevsky Prospect” in his Petersburg Tales.
tossups.question_sanitized: A story by this author ends after a hungover man who promised to host a dinner party is found by his angry guests hiding in the title vehicle. In a story by this author, a man becomes a monk to purify his soul after painting a religious scene in which every figure had demonic eyes. In that story by this author, a painter abandons his style and paints for aristocrats after a bag of money is found in the frame of a cursed portrait of a moneylender. Near the end of a story by this author, a cobbler named (*) Hoffmann helps beat up an officer for dancing with the wife of a tinsmith named Schiller. This author of the collection Arabesques wrote a story in which a painter who takes opium to dream of a dark-haired prostitute is contrasted with Lieutenant Pirogov, who followed a blonde woman down the title street. For 10 points, name this author who included the story "Nevsky Prospect" in his Petersburg Tales.
tossups.answer: Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolai Vasilyevich Gogol] (The first story is “The Carriage.” The story in the second and third sentences is “The Portrait.”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolai Vasilyevich Gogol] (The first story is "The Carriage." The story in the second and third sentences is "The Portrait.")

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a character paints “beauties” for a Persian shopkeeper in exchange for opium to cure his insomnia. No one attends the funeral of an artist, who cuts his throat with a razor after a prostitute declines his marriage proposal, in a story by this author. A story by this author ends with a policeman following a giant-fisted ghost until it confronts him. A “certain clerk” from a “certain department” develops a delirious fever after being berated by a “Person of Consequence” in a story by this author. The title object is wrapped in cloth and thrown off the St. Isaac’s Bridge in a story by this author before it is found in a loaf of bread by a barber. For 10 points, name this author, whose many stories set in St. Petersburg include “Nevsky Prospekt,” “The Nose,” and “The Overcoat.”

For 10 points, name this author, whose many stories set in St. Petersburg include "Nevsky Prospekt," "The Nose," and "The Overcoat."

packet.name: J - Georgia Tech D, McGill B, Minnesota B, Virginia Tech A

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a man anxious about getting married has a dream in which a shopkeeper sells him a hot new type of fabric called “wife.” A fictional version of this author gives the name “Caracas” to an inanimate object that manages to contract syphilis. This writer marries a humanoid balloon in a story titled for his “wife,” by Tommaso Landolfi. This author wrote a story in which a painter buys opium from a Persian, gets high, proposes marriage to a (*) prostitute, and finally slits his throat. The dogs Meggy and Fidel are overheard gossiping in another story by this author, in which the impending accession of Isabella II inspires Poprishchkin (poe-PRISH-kin) to declare himself king of Spain. For 10 points, name this Ukrainian author of fantastical stories like “Nevsky Prospect” and “Diary of a Madman.”
tossups.answer: Nikolay Vasilievich Gogol (The story in the lead-in is “Ivan Fyodorovich Shponka and his Aunt.”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nikolay Vasilievich Gogol (The story in the lead-in is "Ivan Fyodorovich Shponka and his Aunt.")

tossups.question: A critic wrote of this country that “mystic exaltation is not in its nature” in a letter denouncing the author of Selected Passages from Correspondence with My Friends for exalting “affectionate relations...between lords and masters.” A periodical from this modern-day country whose title translates to The Contemporary published a philosophical novel in which Vera has several utopian dreams, including one about the Crystal (*) Palace. A critic from this modern-day country championed the realist paintings of the Wanderers, and also advised a circle of composers that he described as a “mighty handful.” The novel What Is to Be Done? is by an author from, for 10 points, what modern-day country in which nineteenth-century critics rejected the influence of Western Europe in favor of “Slavophilia”?
tossups.answer: Russia [Accept the Russian Federation, Rossiya, Rossiyskaya Federatsiya, prompt on the Soviet Union or U.S.S.R. or Union of Socialist Soviet Republics, CCCP, or Soyúz Sovétskikh Sotsialistícheskikh Respúblik] (The first clue is Belinsky’s letter to Gogol which precipitated him starving himself to death.)

tossups.question: After noting that he has already read the Northern Bee, the narrator of a story by this author then proceeds to call all French people foolish and worthy of a thrashing. That narrator of a story by this author criticizes his coworkers for not going to the theater "unless one throws free tickets at their head." The narrator of one story by this man claims to be the King of Spain before being sent to an asylum; that man is (*) Poprishchin. In a different story by this man, the protagonist, who unironically enjoys copying documents, dies of a fever after being robbed of the title garment. At the end of a story by this author of "Diary of a Madman," a "prominent personage" has his coat stolen by the ghost of Akaky Akakievich. For 10 points, name this Russian author of "The Overcoat" and "The Nose."

For 10 points, name this Russian author of "The Overcoat" and "The Nose."

tossups.question: A story by this author is titled for a cursed object in which a man found 1000 gold coins to pay his rent, which mysteriously disappears from the wall at the story’s end during an auction. In another story by this author, a painter takes opium and slices his throat after learning that the dark-haired beauty he followed was a prostitute. Yet another story by this author of "The Portrait" ends as a watchman sees a phantom with an (*) "enormous mustache" disappear across a bridge. In one of his stories, Pirogorov ("pee-REE-goh-roff") and Piskarev ("pee-SKAR-yoff") part on the title street, while in another, a copyist uses his Christmas bonus to buy the services of the tailor Petrovich to repair the title garment. For 10 points, name this Russian author whose Petersburg Tales include "Nevsky Prospekt" and "The Overcoat".

tossups.question: This author burned the manuscript of the redemptive second volume of one of his novels shortly before dying of self-starvation. A play by this author ends with a “silent scene” in which a postmaster stands “like a question mark” and a pair of similarly-named squires gesticulate furiously at each other. This author ended a novel’s first part by addressing his country as “O Rus’ (“roose”)” and comparing its “headlong motion” to a three-horse carriage or troika. In a play by this author, cronies of a corrupt (*) mayor offer bribes to a man who they mistake for the title incognito (“in-cog-NEET-oh”) bureaucrat. This author of The Inspector-General created a trickster who gambles with Nozdryov (“NOZZ-dree-off”) while travelling the countryside to collect the title items in a scheme to sell deceased peasants for money. For 10 points, name this Russian author who created Chichikov in his novel Dead Souls.

For 10 points, name this Russian author who created Chichikov in his novel Dead Souls.

tossups.question: This author wrote a scene in which a character's introduction receives the response “I've had a bite to eat too” because the character's name, “Yaichnitsa,” means “omelette.” That scene comes from a play by this author in which Kochkaryov's obsessive but incompetent matchmaking results in Agafya being abandoned by Podkolyosin. This author of Marriage wrote a play whose final scene features an enraged man shouting “Who are you laughing at? Laugh at yourselves!” at the audience. Characters in that play by this man include a judge who keeps his hunting equipment in his courtroom named (*) Lyapkin-Tyapkin and a pair of squires named Bobchinsky and Dobchinsky, who mistake the recently-arrived Khlestakov for the title bureaucrat. In a story by this author, the the title object comes to life after being found in a loaf of bread by the barber Yakovlevich. For 10 points, name this Ukrainian-born author of The Inspector-General and the short story “The Nose.”

tossups.question: In a story by this author, two friends start fighting after one of them refuses to give the other his Turkish rifle, calling him a “goose.” In another of his stories, a lieutenant follows a woman home who turns out to be married, while a painter follows a woman who turns out to be a prostitute, and later commits suicide after his marriage proposal is rejected. Those events are bookended by the narrator’s description of the title (*) street. The protagonist of a story by this author makes a derogatory comment about secretaries, provoking a tongue-lashing from the “person of consequence” from whom he has sought help. After that protagonist dies and becomes a ghost, he steals an item of clothing from the general who had reprimanded him. For 10 points, name this author of “How the Two Ivans Quarreled” and “Nevsky Prospect,” who wrote about Akaky Akakievich in “The Overcoat.”

tossups.question: In the preface to one collection, this author mentions a man who pretends to be so cultured that he can no longer remember the common word for rake, but recalls it soon enough when he steps on one. In order to receive gold through enchantment, one of this author’s characters picks a magical red fern blossom. In one story by this author, the devil is offended by a painter’s satirical depiction of him, leading him to hide in coal sacks, along with a deacon and a (*) Cossack. In that story by him, the blacksmith Vakula attempts to woo the vain Oksana by getting her the Tsarina’s slippers. In another story by him, the protagonist cannot remember ritualistically slaughtering the child Ivas to cast a spell to win Ivas’ sister Pidorka. His first collection of stories is introduced by the talkative beekeeper Rudy Panko and includes the stories “Christmas Eve” and “St. John’s Eve.” For 10 points, those stories reflect the Ukrainian heritage of what author of Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka?

This author wrote a story that begins by claiming that “There is nothing more irritable than departments." This author wrote a story in which a philosophy student is made to chant prayers for three nights over the coffin of a witch that he killed. This author’s collection Evenings on a Farm near Dikinka includes the story “May Night.” He wrote a story in which a ghost haunts the “Person of Consequence” whose bullying gave him a fatal fever. For 10 points, name this author of The Government Inspector and Dead Souls.

This author’s bitter dialogue “Leaving the Theater After the Presentation of a New Comedy” stars the guffawing audience members at the premiere of his most famous play, which ends with its characters suddenly freezing in terror. For 10 points, name this author of Taras Bulba whose masterwork is about the theft of Akaky Akakievich’s fancy “Overcoat.”
tossups.question_sanitized: This author described how a young boy who forgot most of his Latin would append the syllable "-us" to the words for random farm implements in a preface narrated by a beekeeper who is named Rudy despite his gray hair. This author wrote about a young man who is brutally murdered by his father for bringing loaves of bread to a starving Polish girl and her mother. Winged demons appear from the corpse of a young girl to haunt the philosopher Khoma Brut in his horror story "Viy." During a visit to the lady Karolina Ivanovna, "a very important person" is haunted by the protagonist of this author's most famous story, a destitute copyist. This author wrote about the father of Ostap and Andriy in a romantic portrayal of the violent Cossack life. For 10 points, name this author of Taras Bulba whose masterwork is about the theft of Akaky Akakievich's fancy "Overcoat."

bonuses.leadin: In a story by this author, Levko correctly identifies a witch from a group of drowned maidens. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In a story by this author, Levko correctly identifies a witch from a group of drowned maidens. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of “May Night.” A witch instructs Petro to decapitate his boss’s son in order to obtain the treasure beneath a magical fern in this author’s story “St. John’s Eve,” which appears in his collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka.

In this Gogol story, Khoma is forced to pray for three nights at the coffin of a Cossack chief’s daughter, who was actually a witch. The title creature of this story is a gnome-like creature with elongated eyelids.
In this other Gogol story, the ghost of Akaky Akakievich haunts the streets of St. Petersburg after he is robbed of the title article of clothing.
parts_sanitized: Name this author of "May Night." A witch instructs Petro to decapitate his boss's son in order to obtain the treasure beneath a magical fern in this author's story "St. John's Eve," which appears in his collection Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka.

In this Gogol story, Khoma is forced to pray for three nights at the coffin of a Cossack chief's daughter, who was actually a witch. The title creature of this story is a gnome-like creature with elongated eyelids.
In this other Gogol story, the ghost of Akaky Akakievich haunts the streets of St. Petersburg after he is robbed of the title article of clothing.

bonuses.leadin: Mikhail Bakhtin introduced “monologic” and “dialogic” subdivisions of this form, distinguishing stylistic agreement between authors and narrators within works in this form. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Mikhail Bakhtin introduced "monologic" and "dialogic" subdivisions of this form, distinguishing stylistic agreement between authors and narrators within works in this form. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Russian literary form first codified by Boris Eikhenbaum. This form is characterized by stories with narration and dialect reminiscent of oral literature and folk tales.
Eikhenbaum first coined the concept of skaz in an essay about this Nikolai Gogol story, in which Akaky Akakievich loses the title garment.

Walter Benjamin’s essay “The Storyteller” praises the skaz stories of this author, such as “The Tale of Cross-eyed Lefty from Tula and the Steel Flea.” A novella by this author analogizes Katerina Ismailova to a Shakespeare villainess.
parts_sanitized: Name this Russian literary form first codified by Boris Eikhenbaum. This form is characterized by stories with narration and dialect reminiscent of oral literature and folk tales.

answers: Nikolai Leskov (The novella is Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District.)
answers_sanitized: "The Overcoat" [or "The Cloak" or "Shinyel"; accept "How Gogol's Overcoat Was Made"]
Nikolai Leskov (The novella is Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District.)

This Italian playwright satirized police incompetence in plays like Accidental Death of an Anarchist.

bonuses.leadin: In this play’s fifth act, a room full of characters are thunderstruck when a Postmaster reads aloud a letter that brutally mocks all of them. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this comedy whose protagonist, a naïve, fairly stupid clerk, takes advantage of a provincial town along with his servant Osip after losing all of his money playing cards.
The Inspector General was written by this Ukrainian-Russian author of “The Overcoat” and Dead Souls.

This Russian directed a landmark 1926 production of The Inspector General where actors emerged from 11 coffin-like doors during a nightmarish bribe scene. He broke from his mentor Stanislavsky (“STAN·iz·slavsky”) to emphasize posture and movement, leading him to develop a school of actor training called Bio·mechanics.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the unique form of literary identity known as the “Russian soul,” for 10 points each.
parts: Vissarion Belinsky coined the term “Russian soul” after reading this author’s novel Dead Souls. Fyodor Dostoevsky quipped that “We all come out from” this author’s story “The Overcoat.”

The Russian Soul titles a book of selections from Dostoevsky’s 1400-page work in this genre that helped to popularize the term. A Turgenev novella framed as a work in this genre created the “superfluous man” archetype.

James Falen’s translation of Eugene Onegin renders the word “khandra” as “Russian soul,” contrasting it with this “English” condition. Pushkin compares Onegin to Childe Harold while describing this sense of melancholy, which sometimes titles the poem’s first canto.
answers: diary [or dnevnik; accept A Writer’s Diary or The Diary of a Superfluous Man; accept Dnevnik pisateli; accept Dnevnik lishnego cheloveka]
answers_sanitized: diary [or dnevnik; accept A Writer's Diary or The Diary of a Superfluous Man; accept Dnevnik pisateli; accept Dnevnik lishnego cheloveka]

parts: Name this story in which an “important personage” prone to verbal repetition propounds a system of “Strictness, strictness, and always strictness!” and rejects a clerk’s appeal for aid.

Eikhenbaum claims that this author of “The Overcoat” determines stories not by plot, but by “some comic situation” that allows for the “elaboration of comic devices.”

bonuses.leadin: In March 2022, many Ukrainians on Instagram criticized Russia by quoting a poet with this first name with lines like “From the Moldavian to the Finn / All silent are in all their tongues.” For 10 points each:
parts: Give this first name of the poet who wrote “The Caucasus.” A character with this first name inspired by Cossack legends tells his son Andriy, “I gave you life, I will also kill you!”
This author changed his novel Taras Bulba in 1842 to remove some Ukrainian themes. This author expounded upon those political views in his novel Dead Souls.

This author praised Gogol as “so unusual…I am [unable] to get over it.” While in Odessa, this author continued a novel with alternating masculine and feminine rhymes whose title character shuns Tatyana and kills a poet in a duel.
answers: Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolay Vasil’yevich Gogol; or Mykola Vasil’ovych Hohol]
Alexander Pushkin [or Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin] (The novel is Eugene Onegin.)
answers_sanitized: Taras [accept Taras (Hryhorovych) Shevchenko or Kobzar Taras or Taras Bulba]
Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolay Vasil'yevich Gogol; or Mykola Vasil'ovych Hohol]
Alexander Pushkin [or Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin] (The novel is Eugene Onegin.)

bonuses.leadin: In a charity performance of this play, Dostoyevsky played a postmaster whose arrival prompts the Mayor to tell the audience, "What are you laughing about? You are laughing at yourselves!" For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 1836 play in which the townspeople offer the conman Khlestakov massive bribes after mistaking him for the title state official.
The Government Inspector is a play by this Russian author, whose other satirical works include short stories like "The Nose" and "The Overcoat."

The plot of The Government Inspector may have inspired this 1956 Swiss play, in which Claire Zachanassian offers a billion francs to her hometown Güllen on the condition that the townspeople kill her lover Anton.
answers: The Visit [or Der Besuch der alten Dame] (by Friedrich Dürrenmatt)
answers_sanitized: The Government Inspector [or The Inspector General or Revizor]
The Visit [or Der Besuch der alten Dame] (by Friedrich Durrenmatt)
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In a charity performance of this play, Dostoyevsky played a postmaster whose arrival prompts the Mayor to tell the audience, "What are you laughing about? You are laughing at yourselves!" For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this 1836 play in which the townspeople offer the conman Khlestakov massive bribes after mistaking him for the title state official.
The Government Inspector is a play by this Russian author, whose other satirical works include short stories like "The Nose" and "The Overcoat."

The plot of The Government Inspector may have inspired this 1956 Swiss play, in which Claire Zachanassian offers a billion francs to her hometown Gullen on the condition that the townspeople kill her lover Anton.

bonuses.leadin: The narrator's growing fear of the villagers in the Wolf Cub Village is exacerbated after seeing these two words. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these two words, which the narrator of that short story wishes to "save the children" from following. "Virtue and morality" is scribbled all over a book where these two words appear in between the lines.

The narrator sees the words "eat people" appear in "Diary of a Madman," a short story by this Chinese author. This author wrote about a man who calls his failures "spiritual victories" in "The True Story of Ah Q."
"Diary of a Madman" takes its title from a short story of the same name by this Russian author. Lu Xun admired this author of "The Nose" and translated his novel "Dead Souls" into Chinese.
answers: Lu Xun [or Lu Sun; or Lu Hsun; accept Zhou Suren]
answers_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Sun; or Lu Hsun; accept Zhou Suren]

bonuses.leadin: Simon Karlinsky analyzed how this author’s repressed sexuality surfaced in his writings in his book The Sexual Labyrinth of [this author]. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this possibly gay Russian author who burned the second part of his only novel a few days before he died horribly from an ascetic lifestyle that was prescribed to rid him of his “inner filth.”

Karlinsky compares the “atrocious tortures” of Gogol’s deathbed treatments to those undergone by Poprishchin, the protagonist of this Gogol story, who convinces himself that he is the king of Spain as he descends into insanity.

One of Gogol’s last letters was to the priest who prescribed him his ascetic lifestyle, asking if they could “exchange” these clothing items. In another Gogol story, Akaky Akakievich is robbed of this title piece of clothing.
answers: Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolai Vasilyevich Gogol] (The novel that he burned was the second part to Dead Souls.)
answers_sanitized: Nikolai Gogol [or Nikolai Vasilyevich Gogol] (The novel that he burned was the second part to Dead Souls.)
overcoats [accept "The Overcoat"; or shinel; accept "The Cloak"; prompt on coats]

One of Gogol's last letters was to the priest who prescribed him his ascetic lifestyle, asking if they could "exchange" these clothing items. In another Gogol story, Akaky Akakievich is robbed of this title piece of clothing.

bonuses.leadin: This story ends with the narrator declaring, “Mother, mother, have pity on your sick child! And do you know that the Bey of Algiers has a wart under his nose?” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this story whose narrator begins signing forms as Ferdinand VII, and describes how he’s being held by the Spanish Inquisition in erratically-dated letters.
This Ukrainian author wrote “The Diary of a Madman.” The barber Ivan Yakovlevich finds his client Major Kovalyov’s nose in his morning bread in this author’s story “The Nose.”
This Italian author wrote the bizarre story “Gogol’s Wife,” which recounts how Gogol married a life-size balloon named Caracas who became pregnant with a balloon child and contracted syphilis.

This Italian author wrote the bizarre story "Gogol's Wife," which recounts how Gogol married a life-size balloon named Caracas who became pregnant with a balloon child and contracted syphilis.

bonuses.leadin: This composer's Sinfonietta was dedicated to the Czechoslovak Army. For 10 points each:
answers: Taras Bulba (It is a novel by Nikolai Gogol.)
parts: Name this composer and Czech nationalist who drew on Moravian folk music for such operas as Jenůfa.

Janáček wrote two pieces for this ensemble, including one inspired by Leo Tolstoy's novella The Kreutzer Sonata. Other Czech pieces for this chamber ensemble include Smetana's ("SMEH-tah-nah'z") "From My Life" and Dvořák's "American" one.

This other work of Russian literature inspired a "symphonic rhapsody" by Janáček which includes a passage scored for brass and organ that represents the title character's "Prophecy". This work also inspired an opera by Mykola Lysenko.
answers_sanitized: Taras Bulba (It is a novel by Nikolai Gogol.)
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This composer's Sinfonietta was dedicated to the Czechoslovak Army. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this composer and Czech nationalist who drew on Moravian folk music for such operas as Jenufa.

Janacek wrote two pieces for this ensemble, including one inspired by Leo Tolstoy's novella The Kreutzer Sonata. Other Czech pieces for this chamber ensemble include Smetana's ("SMEH-tah-nah'z") "From My Life" and Dvorak's "American" one.

This other work of Russian literature inspired a "symphonic rhapsody" by Janacek which includes a passage scored for brass and organ that represents the title character's "Prophecy". This work also inspired an opera by Mykola Lysenko.

bonuses.leadin: This character is disturbed while praying “with an expression of profound piety” in a cathedral by an official who is himself distracted by a girl in a white dress. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character who is seen exiting a carriage with a sheathed sword and wearing a gold-braided uniform with a collar that obscures his face. The story begins with a barber finding this character in a loaf of bread and throwing him in a river.
Major Kovalyov’s nose falls off in a short story by this 19th-century Russian author, who also wrote “The Overcoat” and the novel Dead Souls.

In this Gogol story, a witch forces the philosophy student Khoma to carry her and chant hymns over her corpse. He later dies in fright of the fiends summoned by the title demon, who has eyelids that touch the floor.

bonuses.leadin: Danes celebrate this holiday by lighting a bonfire with an effigy of a witch on top. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this holiday in June often associated with the production of medicinal plants and the Midsummer. In Lithuania, people search for the magic fern blossom and worshipped Rasa prior to the Christianization of the Baltics.

Witches are a common theme in St. John’s Eve celebrations, exemplified by this Ukranian author who wrote about a witch appearing to Petro in a story titled for the holiday. He also wrote “The Nose” and “The Overcoat”.

People in this country celebrate St. John’s Eve by hitting their neighbors on the head with toy hammers and a leek. This country celebrates the dragon Coco by staging a fight between him and St. George during the Corpus Christi celebrations.
answers_sanitized: St. John's Eve [or Saint Jonas' festival; or Sankthansaften]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Danes celebrate this holiday by lighting a bonfire with an effigy of a witch on top. For 10 points each:

People in this country celebrate St. John's Eve by hitting their neighbors on the head with toy hammers and a leek. This country celebrates the dragon Coco by staging a fight between him and St. George during the Corpus Christi celebrations.

bonuses.leadin: One woman in this play is courted by a man whose name translates to omelet and a man who wants her to speak French, even though he doesn’t speak it. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play, the protagonist of which jumps out of a window to avoid the title event.
The final scene of this play includes an angry man, who was tricked by the title character, shouting “Who are you laughing at? Laugh at yourselves!” at the audience.
This Russian playwright wrote both Marriage and The Inspector General.
answers_sanitized: The Inspector General [or The Government Inspector; or Revizor]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: One woman in this play is courted by a man whose name translates to omelet and a man who wants her to speak French, even though he doesn't speak it. For 10 points each:

This Russian playwright wrote both Marriage and The Inspector General.

bonuses.leadin: An author was exiled from this country for writing a 1790 study of its social issues titled for a ‘Journey’ between its two largest cities. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country. Another author satirised political corruption in this country in a play in which a civil servant poses as the title figure and flees before the Postmaster delivers a letter revealing him to be a fraud.

This Russian author was exiled to New Russia after calling Alexander I a ‘wandering despot’ with a ‘plump posterior’. He was later fatally wounded in a duel, like Lensky in his verse novel Eugene Onegin.

Pushkin recalled seeing the coffin of this other Russian writer while travelling in the Caucasus. This author of the censored verse comedy Woe from Wit was lynched and torn apart by locals after being appointed Russian minister in Tehran.
answers: Imperial Russia [or Rossiya; the works mentioned are Journey from Petersburg to Moscow by Radishchev and A Government Inspector by Gogol]
answers_sanitized: Imperial Russia [or Rossiya; the works mentioned are Journey from Petersburg to Moscow by Radishchev and A Government Inspector by Gogol]
